mnemonics: PIP: Primitives are Passed, Objects are Referenced (but the reference itself is passed by value)
Think of a Dog & Leash: You get a copy of the leash (reference), not the dog (object). You can't change which dog the leash is attached to, but you can tell the dog to sit.
keyPoints: Java is always Pass-by-Value. The value being passed is either the primitive itself or a copy of the object reference.
When you pass a primitive (int, double, boolean), a copy of its value is made. Changes inside the method do not affect the original variable.

When you pass an object (e.g., an array or a StringBuffer), a copy of the reference (the 'memory address') is passed. You can use this reference to modify the object's state, but you cannot change the original reference to point to a new object.
commonMistakes: Believing objects are passed 'by reference'. They are not; the reference to the object is passed by value.

Assuming that changing an object's state inside a method (e.g., using a setter) is the same as reassigning the parameter (e.g., `parameter = new Object()`). The former changes the original object; the latter only changes the local copy of the reference and has no effect on the caller's variable.
